ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes two distinct architectural ideas — an extra gate to weight passage words by question relevance and a self-matching attention to aggregate passage-wide evidence — that are not mere rehashes of BiDAF/Match-LSTM and are clearly differentiated in the writeup. These extensions constitute non-trivial modifications to existing attention-based RNNs and the combination with pointer networks yields empirical gains on SQuAD, so an expert would find the design choices informative rather than purely incremental. That said, the contributions are still evolutionary within the now well-studied reading-comprehension design space (attention + RNN + pointer), and I note no formal/theoretical justification for why the added gate or self-matching should provably improve representations, which limits the sense of radical novelty. | B(4): The paper proposes two architecture-level innovations — a gated attention mechanism that weights passage words by question relevance and a self-matching attention that aggregates evidence across the passage — combined with question-initialized pointer decoding. These ideas constitute a clear, non-trivial extension of attention-based RNN QA models (and complement pointer-network answer span prediction) rather than a mere rehash of prior baselines like Match-LSTM or BiDAF. The contributions are well-differentiated from related work in the summary and would likely inform practitioners seeking modular improvements for SQuAD-style reading comprehension.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes two distinct architectural ideas — an extra gate to weight passage words by question relevance and a self-matching attention to aggregate passage-wide evidence — that are not mere rehashes of BiDAF/Match-LSTM and are clearly differentiated in the writeup. These extensions constitute non-trivial modifications to existing attention-based RNNs and the combination with pointer networks yields empirical gains on SQuAD, so an expert would find the design choices informative rather than purely incremental. That said, the contributions are still evolutionary within the now well-studied reading-comprehension design space (attention + RNN + pointer), and I note no formal/theoretical justification for why the added gate or self-matching should provably improve representations, which limits the sense of radical novelty.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes two architecture-level innovations — a gated attention mechanism that weights passage words by question relevance and a self-matching attention that aggregates evidence across the passage — combined with question-initialized pointer decoding. These ideas constitute a clear, non-trivial extension of attention-based RNN QA models (and complement pointer-network answer span prediction) rather than a mere rehash of prior baselines like Match-LSTM or BiDAF. The contributions are well-differentiated from related work in the summary and would likely inform practitioners seeking modular improvements for SQuAD-style reading comprehension.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to a comprehensive set of recent SQuAD baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, DCN, RaSoR, etc.) using the same dataset and standard EM/F1 metrics, which supports fair empirical comparison. However, the authors do not appear to acknowledge limitations or cases where prior methods perform comparably, and the summary lacks evidence of careful ablation or causal analysis isolating the proposed gated/self-matching components versus baselines. Related work coverage is strong, but the comparative analysis falls short of the rigorous, component-level disambiguation I expect, so I deduct one point. | B(4): The paper compares against a comprehensive set of recent SQuAD baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM+Ans-Ptr, DCN, RaSoR, etc.) and evaluates on the same dataset with standard EM/F1 metrics, indicating fair experimental conditions. Related work coverage is thorough and hyperparameters are reported, supporting reproducibility. However, the summary does not explicitly state ablation analyses or clear acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably on particular subsets, so while comparisons are strong they lack a few clarifying analyses that would make them fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to a comprehensive set of recent SQuAD baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, DCN, RaSoR, etc.) using the same dataset and standard EM/F1 metrics, which supports fair empirical comparison. However, the authors do not appear to acknowledge limitations or cases where prior methods perform comparably, and the summary lacks evidence of careful ablation or causal analysis isolating the proposed gated/self-matching components versus baselines. Related work coverage is strong, but the comparative analysis falls short of the rigorous, component-level disambiguation I expect, so I deduct one point.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a comprehensive set of recent SQuAD baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM+Ans-Ptr, DCN, RaSoR, etc.) and evaluates on the same dataset with standard EM/F1 metrics, indicating fair experimental conditions. Related work coverage is thorough and hyperparameters are reported, supporting reproducibility. However, the summary does not explicitly state ablation analyses or clear acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably on particular subsets, so while comparisons are strong they lack a few clarifying analyses that would make them fully comprehensive.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized with a clear flow from motivation through model architecture to experiments, and the main contributions (gated attention, self-matching attention, pointer-based span prediction) are stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and accompanied by thorough related-work discussion and reported hyperparameters, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples are effective enough to support understanding, and overall writing quality is good. Minor presentation issues remain (e.g., no explicit discussion of limitations in the manuscript and limited mention of released code), which would have helped delimit scope and replication. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from method (gated-attention RNN + self-matching attention + pointer network) to experiments on SQuAD and a concise conclusion; the main contributions are stated early and the experimental details (including hyperparameters) are reported. Notation appears consistent and related work coverage is thorough, and the writing quality is described as good, making the presentation easy to follow for practitioners. Minor presentation omissions—most notably that no limitations are stated and code availability is not mentioned—prevent a top score, but overall clarity and informativeness are strong.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized with a clear flow from motivation through model architecture to experiments, and the main contributions (gated attention, self-matching attention, pointer-based span prediction) are stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and accompanied by thorough related-work discussion and reported hyperparameters, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples are effective enough to support understanding, and overall writing quality is good. Minor presentation issues remain (e.g., no explicit discussion of limitations in the manuscript and limited mention of released code), which would have helped delimit scope and replication.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from method (gated-attention RNN + self-matching attention + pointer network) to experiments on SQuAD and a concise conclusion; the main contributions are stated early and the experimental details (including hyperparameters) are reported. Notation appears consistent and related work coverage is thorough, and the writing quality is described as good, making the presentation easy to follow for practitioners. Minor presentation omissions—most notably that no limitations are stated and code availability is not mentioned—prevent a top score, but overall clarity and informativeness are strong.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a strong, topical empirical contribution to reading-comprehension and QA with appropriate baselines, metrics, and clear relevance to the NLP/ML community. Reviewer A’s concerns about deeper theoretical justification and more explicit component-level ablations are valid but relatively minor given the solid empirical results and reported experimental details noted by Reviewer B, so the paper is judged appropriate for a top venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses reading comprehension on SQuAD using novel gated-attention and self-matching mechanisms and compares to appropriate baselines with standard EM/F1 metrics, so topicality and evaluation fit a top NLP/ML venue. From my theory-focused perspective, the work appears primarily empirical and the summary lacks any formal justification or explicit ablation evidence isolating the new gate and self-matching components (and code release is not mentioned), which are minor concerns for a top conference expecting deeper analysis and reproducibility. Overall it is a strong empirical contribution for this community but would be stronger with clearer causal/theoretical motivation and component-level evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML conference: it tackles reading comprehension on SQuAD with practical architectural innovations (gated attention and self-matching) and integrates them with pointer networks to produce state-of-the-art single-model and ensemble results. The methodology, baselines, metrics (EM/F1), and thorough experimental details (including hyperparameters and related work) align with the empirical and reproducibility standards expected at premier venues. Given its clear relevance to applied QA research and strong empirical validation, it targets the right audience and is positioned well within the community.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers strong empirical results on SQuAD with thorough baselines, reported hyperparameters, and solid related work coverage. They also both note the lack of explicit ablation/sensitivity analyses for the gated/self-matching components, absent discussion of limitations/failure cases, and no stated code release. Given the convincing empirical performance but missing diagnostic experiments and discussion, a score of 4 reflects practical strength while flagging important gaps to address.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents strong empirical comparisons on SQuAD against many relevant baselines and reports hyperparameters, supporting the main performance claims. However, the summary does not indicate targeted ablation studies isolating the added gate and the self-matching module or any analysis proving why these components improve representations, which I require as a theory-focused reviewer. Related work coverage is thorough, but no failure cases or limitations are discussed and code release is not mentioned, limiting reproducibility and interpretability of the reported SOTA gains.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents strong empirical evaluation on SQuAD with comparisons to a wide range of competitive baselines and reports hyperparameters, which supports the main performance claims. However the summary does not mention explicit ablation studies or sensitivity analyses for the gated/self-matching modules, and no limitations or failure cases are discussed; code availability is also not stated. Related work coverage appears thorough. Overall the experiments are solid and practically convincing but missing some diagnostic analyses and discussion of limitations.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important problem and achieves strong empirical results with practical, modular architectural contributions (gated attention and self-matching) that are likely to influence applied QA practice. While the work is primarily empirical rather than theoretical and lacks deep formal justification or extensive isolating analyses, its clear practical impact and usefulness to practitioners justify a slightly above-average score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets an important problem (span-based reading comprehension on SQuAD) and presents practical architectural innovations (an extra gate on attention-based RNNs and a self-matching attention layer) that yielded single-model and ensemble state-of-the-art performance. However, from a theory-focused perspective the contributions appear primarily empirical and engineering-driven: the summary lists no formal justification for the new gate or self-matching mechanism and does not report whether thorough ablation studies isolate each design choice, which limits how much it will shift researchers’ conceptual understanding. Thus the work is practically useful and likely to be built upon, but is not transformative or broadly paradigm-changing.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles the highly important problem of machine reading comprehension and delivers strong empirical gains on SQuAD (single-model and ensemble state-of-the-art at submission) using practical, modular components: gated attention, self-matching attention, and pointer-network decoding. From a practitioner’s perspective these architectural additions are straightforward to integrate, well-validated with hyperparameter details, and likely to influence subsequent QA systems and engineering practice. While not radically theoretical, the work shapes how researchers aggregate long-range passage evidence and initialize answer decoding, opening useful follow-up directions for applied QA.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents clear, coherent architectural contributions and strong empirical results on SQuAD with reported hyperparameters against competitive baselines. The main shortcomings are absence of theoretical justification, limited ablation/disentangling of components, and no mention of code release or statistical testing; these are important but do not invalidate the experimental evidence. Given the technical coherence and substantial empirical support, a score of 4 reflects solid soundness with some room for more rigorous validation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims (gated attention + self-matching attention + pointer networks achieving SQuAD SOTA) and compares against appropriate baselines with reported hyperparameters, which supports the experimental claim. However, from the summary there is no theoretical analysis justifying why an extra gate or self-matching should provably improve representations, nor is there mention of ablation studies that isolate the gated attention and self-matching components or of released code for reproducibility. These omissions raise concerns about whether the improvements stem from the proposed mechanisms versus other implementation or tuning details, so the evidence is incomplete though not obviously invalid.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims (gated attention, self-matching attention, and pointer-network span prediction) are clearly described and supported by empirical results on SQuAD against strong baselines, with hyperparameters reported and competitive single-model and ensemble scores. There are no formal mathematical proofs needed for the architecture, and the methodology—bidirectional RNN encoders, gated-attention, self-matching, and question-initialized pointer decoding—is technically coherent. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, code release, or explicit discussion of limitations, which are minor gaps that prevent a full technical-flawlessness rating. Overall the experimental validation is strong enough to justify the conclusions.

main_claims: Introduce a gated attention-based recurrent network that adds an extra gate to attention-based RNNs to weight passage words by question relevance.
Propose a self-matching attention mechanism that matches the passage against itself to aggregate evidence from the whole passage and refine passage representations.
Combine the gated attention and self-matching with pointer networks to predict answer span boundaries and achieve state-of-the-art results on SQuAD at time of submission (single-model and ensemble).
_summary.methodology: Process question and passage with bidirectional RNNs, apply a gated attention-based recurrent network to produce question-aware passage representations, refine those with self-matching attention over the passage, and use pointer networks (with question-initialized hidden state) to predict start and end span positions.

reviewer_a.name: Theory-Focused Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning and theoretical NLP, with publications on model interpretability and formal analyses of neural architectures.
traits: Penalizes papers that lack formal or theoretical justification for novel architectural components (e.g., no analysis explaining why an extra gate or self-matching provably improves representation).
Requires thorough ablation studies that isolate each design choice; will deduct points if key components aren't individually evaluated or if interaction effects are unclear.
Downweights incremental empirical gains: will be critical of small SOTA improvements especially when achieved mainly via ensembles rather than single-model innovations.
Penalizes lack of reproducibility artifacts (e.g., no released code, hyperparameters, or training details sufficient to replicate results).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I prioritize theoretical motivation, clear causal evidence for claimed mechanisms, and reproducibility; I penalize primarily empirical papers that add engineering tweaks without deeper analysis.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Empirical Advocate
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in applied NLP with extensive experience building state-of-the-art QA systems and running large-scale empirical evaluations on benchmarks like SQuAD.
traits: Rewards clear empirical improvements on standard benchmarks (e.g., single-model and ensemble SQuAD results) and practical performance gains.
Values well-executed architectural innovations that are empirically validated; will upscore papers with solid ablation tables and competitive baselines.
Prefers papers that demonstrate engineering maturity (e.g., careful training regimes, pointer-network integration, question-initialized decoding) even if lacking formal proofs.

Favors papers that present intuitive, modular ideas likely to be adopted by practitioners (e.g., gated-attention modules, self-matching) and provides sufficient experimental detail to reproduce performance.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I judge papers mainly on empirical novelty, thorough experiments, and practical impact; I reward architectures that deliver clear gains and likely real-world utility, even without formal analysis.
